node.title: I had questions about system design round.

article.title: I had questions about system design round.
article.content: Quick question — in system design interviews, when you recommend a specific database (e.g. DynamoDB vs Cassandra), how much depth is expected? Should we know the internals, or is it sufficient to understand the use cases, tradeoffs, and high-level design decisions?

post.content:
In most interviews, I'd expect to be challenged more on why you picked it than on how it's implemented internally.

If you say DynamoDB, the interviewer is usually more interested in hearing things like predictable latency, operational overhead, scaling characteristics, partitioning concerns, consistency tradeoffs, etc.

That said, knowing a little about the internals helps because it gives credibility to those tradeoffs. You don't necessarily need to explain DynamoDB's storage engine, but you should have some intuition for why it behaves the way it does.

A good rule of thumb is: if someone asked "why not Cassandra?" after your recommendation, could you defend your choice beyond "that's what companies use"? If yes, you're probably at the right depth.

One idea that really changed how I think about databases came from a system design video: **when you choose a database, you're essentially choosing a data structure that best fits your application's access patterns and requirements.**

For example, a relational database, a key-value store, a document database, and a graph database are all optimized for different types of data access and trade-offs. Understanding the underlying data structures, indexing mechanisms, storage engines, and consistency models helps you make better architectural decisions rather than selecting a database based solely on popularity.

To build this intuition, spend some time learning how databases work internally. Understanding concepts such as B-Trees, LSM Trees, replication, partitioning, caching, and transaction management will significantly improve your system design skills.

A great way to deepen your understanding is by reading engineering white papers. 
* Amazon Aurora (best for starting, get the idea of compute and storage layers)
* Amazon DynamoDB
* Apache Kafka (I am also reading)

While reading these papers, focus less on implementation details and more on the problems being solved, the trade-offs considered, and why the final design was chosen. This mindset will help you approach system design problems more effectively in both interviews and real-world engineering work.
Go deep as you need based on use case.
